TOC.title: Contents

para.p:
Let
:
f ,
f ′
→ X Y
be two maps. We say that
f
is homotopic to
f ′
if there exists a continuous map
: F
→
× X I
Y
such that:

Math.text: for-all@(x) element-of X colon F * open-interval@(x, 0) = f * x * [ and ] * F * open-interval@(x, 1) = f ^ prime * x

para.p:
and the map
f
is said to be homotopic to
f
, written
≃ f
f ′
.

Two paths
:
f , g
→
[ 0 , 1 ]
X
with same start and end points,say
a , b
respectively are said to be path homotopic if there exists a homotopy
: F
→
× I I
X
such that

Math.text: for-all@(t) element-of closed-interval@(0, 1) colon formulae@(F * open-interval@(0, t) = a, F * open-interval@(1, t) = b)

para.p:
and we write
≃ p

The relations
≃
and
≃ p
are equivalence relations

Let
:
f , g
→
[ 0 , 1 ]
X
be paths in
X
, such that
=
⁢ f
( 1 )
⁢ g
( 0 )
. We define the product
⋆ f g
of the paths to by

The product operation defines a well defined operation on path homotopy classes. I.e, if
[ f ]
denotes the path homotopy class of
f
, then
:=
⋆
[ g ]
[ h ]
[
⋆ g h
]
is a well defined operation on path homotopy classes
Furthermore, find an ”identity” path, and show that the product operation satisfies the group axioms
A path starting and ending at a point is said to be a loop based at that point.

Let
X
be a topological space, and let
∈
x 0
X
. The set of all path homotopy classes of loops based at
x 0
is a group, denoted
⁢
π 1
( X ,
x 0
)
and called the first fundamental group (or just the fundamental group) at
x 0
.

p.text:
Let
α
be a path from
x 0
to
x 1
and define a map

Math.text: hat@(alpha) colon pi _ 1 * open-interval@(X, x _ 0) rightarrow pi _ 1 * open-interval@(X, x _ 1)

p.text: by

Math.text: delimited-[]@(f) maps-to (delimited-[]@(alpha)) ^ (- 1) star delimited-[]@(f) star delimited-[]@(alpha)

p.text:
where
[ α ]
- 1
is the path homotopy class of the reverse path from
x 1
to
x 0
. The map
^ α
is a group homomorphism. Furthermore, it is an isomorphism.

para.p: exercise. ∎

Note that the maps
^ α
are not canonical isomorphism, as they reqire the choice of a path
α
. Also, when
=
x 0
x 1
,
^ α
is an inner automorphism.

p.text:
If
X
is path connected and
∈
x 0
,
x 1
X
, we have that
≅
⁢
π 1
( X ,
x 0
)
⁢
π 1
( X ,
x 1
)
. So it makes sense to talk about the fundamental group of a path connected space.

para.p:
A path connected space
X
is said to be simply connected if for some
∈
x 0
X
,
=
⁢
π 1
( X ,
x 0
)
0
and hence for every
∈ x X
.

The category consisting of pairs
( X ,
x 0
)
as objects and continuous maps
: f
→ X Y
such that
=
⁢ f
(
x 0
)
y 0
as morphisms between
( X ,
x 0

to
( Y ,
y 0
)
is called the category of pointed topological sapces, denoted
Top ⋆
.

Let
: ϕ
→
( X ,
x 0
)
( Y ,
y 0
)
be a map of pointed topological spaces. Define the map

Math.text: phi _ star colon pi _ 1 * open-interval@(X, x _ 0) rightarrow pi _ 1 * open-interval@(Y, y _ 0)

para.p:
by
↦
[ f ]
[
∘ ϕ f
]
. This map is said to be the homomorphism induced by
ϕ
.
Show that the induced homomorphism is indeed a group homomorphism

subsection.title: I Functorial properties of the induced homomorphism

=
(

( X ,

( X ,

=
i ⋆
id
⁢

( X ,

)
.

para.p: exercise ∎

All of this is to say that taking the fundamental group at a point is a functor
→
Top ⋆
Grp
.

p.text:
If
: h
→
( X ,

( Y ,
y 0
)

:
h ⋆

⁢

( X ,

)

)
is an isomorphism.

para.p: (The above holds for any functor, but we state it explicitly anyways.)

Let
: p
→ E B
be a surjective map of topological spaces. The open set
U
of
B
is said to be evenly covered by
B
if
⁢
p
- 1
( U )
can be written as the disjoint union of open sets
V α
such that the restriction of
p
to
V α
is a homeomorphism onto
U
. The collection
{
V α
}
will be called a partition of
⁢
p
- 1
( U )
into slices

Let
: p
→ E B
be a surjective map of topological sapces. If for every
∈ b B
, there is an open
⊃ U
{ b }
such that
U
is evenly covered by
p
, then
E
is said to be a covering space for
B
and
p
is said to be a covering map .

That is to say, if
B
is locally evenly covered by
p
, then
E
is a covering space for
B
.
Show that the above definition of covering space is equivalent to the definition of covering space derived from the following definition for evenly covered set:

: p
→ E B
surjective map of topological spaces,
⊆ U B
is said to be evenly covered if there exists a set
A
with the discrete topology such that there exists a homeomorphism
: φ
→
× U A
⁢
p
- 1
( U )
making the following diagram commutative:

The real line with the map
↦ x
(
cos
( x )
,
sin
( x )
)
is a covering map
→ R
S 1
, and
R
is thus a covering space for
S 1
.

A space is always a covering space of itself, with covering map identity. However, in the case of Example , there are more maps than just the identity. Namely those given by
↦
(
cos
( x )
,
sin
( x )
)
(
cos
(
⁢ n x
)
,
sin
(
⁢ n x
)
)
, one for each integer
n
. Now, visually speaking, the fundamental group of
S 1
seems to be what? Does this hint to some sort of Galois Correspondence for convering maps and subgroups of the fundamental group given sufficiently ”good” conditions?
Show that covering maps are open maps.

Let
: p
→ E B
be a covering map, and let
⊆
B 0
B
be a subspace. Show that
:
p |
⁢
p
- 1
(
B 0
)
→
⁢
p
- 1
(
B 0
)
B 0
is a covering map.
product of covering maps is a covering map

Define a figure 8 to be two non degenerate circles meeting at a single common point. Show that the ’infinite grid’
∪
× R Z
× Z R
is a covering space of the figure 8. (Hint: You should be able to show this without needing to explicitly write down the map
p
by using the previous exercise(s). You can also write down the map explicitly if you want, simply use the maps in Example )
How do coverings relate to the fundamental group? we make this connection using lifting.

Let
: p
→ E B
be a covering map. A lift of
: f
→ X B
along
p
is a map
~ f
such that
=
∘ p
~ f
f
.

subsection.title: I Lifting Properties of Covering Spaces

p.text:
Let
: p
→ E B
be a covering map, and let
: f
→
[ 0 , 1 ]
B
be a path with
=
⁢ f
( 0 )
b
. If
∈ e
⁢
p
- 1
( b )
, then there is a unique lift
:
~ f
→
[ 0 , 1 ]
E
with
=
⁢
~ f
( 0 )
e
.

para.p:
Since
p
is a covering map, there exists an open cover
{
U α
}
∈ α J
of
B
such that each
U α
is evenly covered by
p
. Now, note that
{
⁢
f
- 1
(
U α
)
}
∈ α J
is an open cover of
[ 0 , 1 ]
, and by the lebesgue number lemma, there is a
> δ 0
with the property that for any
∈ x
[ 0 , 1 ]
, we have
⊆
( x ,
+ x δ
)
⁢
f
- 1
(
U α
)
for some
∈ α J
. Hence we can find a finite subdivision
0 =
s 0
<
s 1
< ⋯ <
s n
= 1
such that
⊆
⁢ f
(
[
s i
,
s
+ i 1
]
)
U α
for some
∈ α J
.

Define
=
⁢
~ f
( 0 )
e
, and assume that
~ f
has been continuously defined on
[ 0 ,
s i
]
for some
≥ i 0
. We extend
~ f
continuously to
[ 0 ,
s
+ i 1
]
. In order to do so, note that
⊆
⁢ f
(
[
s i
,
s
+ i 1
]
)
U α
for some
∈ α J
, and
=
⁢
p
- 1
(
U α
)
⨆
∈ j I

V j
⋯
( 1 )
for some open
V j
with
:
p |

→
V j
U α
a homeomorphism for each
∈ j I
. Pick the unique
j
with
∈
⁢
~ f
(
s i
)
V j
. Then using (1) we have that
=
⁢
~ f
( s )
⁢
p |
V j
- 1
(
⁢ f
( s )
)
is a continuous extension to
[ 0 ,
s
+ i 1
]
by the pasting lemma. ∎
The above theorem shows that paths lift uniquely to other paths in the covering space given the starting point of the lifted path.

Let
: p
→ E B
be a covering map and let
: F
→
×
[ 0 , 1 ]
[ 0 , 1 ]
B
be a continuous map with
=
⁢ F
(
( 0 , 0 )
)
b

∈ e
⁢
F
- 1
( b )

:
~ F
→
×
[ 0 , 1 ]
[ 0 , 1 ]
E
with
=
⁢
~ F
(
( 0 , 0 )
)
e
. (Hint: just repeat the previous proof, making necessary changes along the way.)
Use the previous exercise to show that homotopies (resp. path homotopies) ”lift” to homotopies (resp. path homotopies) uniquely
Note that loops need not lift to loops (this should be clear by visualising the map in Example )

But, all hope is not lost. Given a loop and starting point for the lift, the ending point is uniquely determined. So, given
∈
b 0
B
and a point
∈
e 0
⁢
p
- 1
(
b 0
)
we have a map

Math.text: phi colon pi _ 1 * open-interval@(B, b _ 0) rightarrow p ^ (- 1) * b _ 0

. Note that this map is not canonical, since it depends on the choice of
e 0

the map
: ϕ
→

π 1
( B ,
b 0
)

(
b 0

)

p.text:
w.r.t
e 0
is surjective. If
E
is simply connected, it is bijective.

para.p: exercise. ∎

p.text: We have the isomorphism

para.p:
The map
↦ t
(
cos
(
⁢ 2 π t
)
,
sin
(
⁢ 2 π t
)
)
is a covering map, and we take the lifting correspondence
: ϕ
⁢
π 1
(
S 1
)
≅
⁢
π 1
(
S 1
,
( 0 , 1 )
)
→
⁢
p
- 1
(
( 0 , 1 )
)
= Z
w.r.t
0
. This map is certainly bijective, since
R
is simply connected. We need to show that this map is a homomorphism. To that end, let
=
⁢ ϕ
(
[ f ]
)
m
,
=
⁢ ϕ
(
[ g ]
)
n
. Note that if
~ f
,
~ g
are the unique lifts of
f , g
along
p
, then
⋆
~ f
(
+ m
~ g
)
goes from
0
to
+ m n
, and a simple computation shows that it is a lifting of
⋆ f g
along
p
. Thus
⁢ ϕ
(
⋆
[ f ]
[ g ]
)
=
+ m n
=
+
⁢ ϕ
(
[ f ]
)
⁢ ϕ
(
[ g ]
)
. ∎

Let
X
be a topological space. For
⊆ A X
, a retraction
: r
→ X A
is a map of topological spaces such that
=
∘ r
i A
⁢ i
d A
where
:
i A
↪ A X
is the inclusion. If for a subspace
A
there exists such an
r
, we say that
A
is a retract of
X
.

If
A
is a retract of
X
, and
∈
a 0
A
then the induced map
:
(
i A
)
⋆
→
⁢
π 1
( A ,
a 0
)
⁢
π 1
( X ,
a 0
)
is an injection. (What does it say about
r ⋆
?)

is not a retract of
B 2

=
B 2

1

theorem.title: Theorem 4.3 (Brouwer’s fixed point theorem for 2 dimensions).

p.text:
Let
: f
→
B 2
B 2
be a continuous mapping. Then
f
has a fixed point.

para.p:
We assume that there is a map
: f
→
B 2
B 2
with no fixed points for contradcition. Thus,
:
∈
∀ x
B 2
≠ x
⁢ f
( x )
, so we can pick
>
t x
0
such that
=
|
+ x
⁢
(
-
⁢ f
( x )
x
)
t x
|
1
. Now define

Math.text: h * x = f * x + t _ x * (x - f * x)

para.p:
and note that
t x
varies continuously with
x
, because it can be solved for using the quadratic formula. Thus
h
is continuous, and we also have that
=
⁢ h
( x )
x
for all
∈ x
S 1
. Thus we have a retraction
: h
→
B 2
S 1
, a contradcition. ∎
prove the one dimensional version of the above theorem.

Let
: f
→ X Y
be a surjective function, where
X
is a topological space. The quotient topology on
Y
induced by
X , f
is the collection of subsets
⊆ V Y
such that
⁢
f
- 1
( V )
is open in
X
.
verify that this is indeed a topology.

a surjective function
: f
→ X Y
is said to be a quotient map if
Y
has the quotient topology from
X , f
.
note that a quotient map is continuous.

p.text:
Let
: f
→ X Y
be a function. Then the quotient topology on
Y
induced by
X , f
is the finest topology with respect to which
f
is continuous.

para.p: exercise. ∎

A surjective function
: f
→ X Y
is a quotient map iff the following condition holds:A subset
K
is closed in
Y
iff
⁢
f
- 1
( K )
is closed in
X

p.text:
if
: f
→ X Y
is a surjective open or closed map, then
f
is a quotient map.

Let
: f
→ X Y
be a quotient map. Let
⊆ B Y
and
A =
⁢
f
- 1
( B )
⊆ X
be subspaces and let
: g
→ A B
be the restricted map.
If
is open or closed, then
is a quotient map
If
is an open or closed map, then
is a quotient map.

para.p: exercise ∎
Perhaps the most important property of quotient maps is the following:

p.text:
Let
: f
→ X Y
be a quotient map, and let
Z
be a top. sp. Let
: h
→ X Z
be a function such that
=
⁢ h
( x )
⁢ h
(
x ′
)
whenever
=
⁢ f
( x )
⁢ f
(
x ′
)
. Then
h
induces a unique function
: g
→ Y Z
such that
= h
∘ g f
. The function
g
is continuous iff
h
is continuous. The function
g
is a quotient map iff
h
is a quotient map.

para.p: These are not hard to prove, but they are results to keep in mind.

p.text:
Let
: h
→ X Z
be a continuous surjective map. Let
∼
be the equivalence relation
∼ x y
iff
=
⁢ h
( x )
⁢ h
( y )
, and let
X / ∼
. Give
X / ∼
the quotient topology from the canonical surjection
π : X → X / ∼
.
the map
induces a continuous bijective map
the above map is a homeomorphism iff
is a quotient map.
If
is hausdorff, so is

para.p:
Let
X
be a topological space, and
U , V
open subsets of
X
with the property that
= X
∪ U V
, and
≠
∩ U V
∅
. The four inclusion maps: [insert figure] induce fundamental group homomorphisms [insert figure] By the universal property of the free product, there exists a homomorphism
: Φ
⁢
⋆
⁢
π 1
( U , p )
π 1
( X , p )
such that the following diagram commutes [insert figure]

p.text:
Let
X
be a top. sp. Suppose that
⊆
U , V
X
are open with
= X
∪ U V
,
U
and
V
path connected. Let
∈ p
∩ U V
, and define
⊆ C
⁢
⋆
⁢
π 1
( U , p )
π 1
( V , p )
by

Math.text: C = conditional-set@(i _ star * gamma * j _ star * gamma, gamma element-of pi _ 1 * open-interval@(U intersection V, p))

p.text:
Then the map
Φ
is surjective, its kernel is the normal closure of
C
in
⁢
⋆
⁢
π 1
( U , p )
π 1
( V , p )
. Therefore,
≅
⁢
π 1
( X , p )
/
(
⁢
⋆
⁢
π 1
( U , p )
π 1
( V , p )
)
¯ C

para.p:
Let
f , h
be group homomorphisms
→ H
G 1
,
→ H
G 2
respectively. The amalgamated free product of
G 1
,
G 2
along
H
is denoted by
⋆ H
G 1
G 2
, is the quotient group
/
(
⋆
G 1
G 2
)
¯ C
where
C
is the set
{
⁢ f
( g )
h
- 1
( g )
:
∈ g H
}
, thought of as a subset of
⋆
G 1
G 2
by means of the usual inclusions of
G 1
,
G 2
into the free product.

therefore, the van kampen theorem states that
≅
⁢
π 1
( X , p )
⁢
⋆
⁢
π 1
(
∩ U V
, p )
⁢
π 1
( U , p )
π 1
( V , p )
. I wont be providing proofs, since they will just be copy pastes of [] .

The wedge sum
∨
X 1
⋯
X n
of topological spaces is defined as the quotient of
⨆ j
X j
by the equivalence relation generated by
⁢
p 1
~ ⋯
~ p
n
. . Let
⋆
be the point in
∨
X 1
⋯
X n
that is the equivalence class of the points
p i
.

a point
∈ p X
is said to be a nondegenerate base point if there exists a neighbourhood
W
of
p
that admits a strong deformation retract onto
p
.
Note for example, that every point in a manifold is a nondegenerate base point.

p.text:
Suppose that
∈
p i
X i
are nondegenerate base points for every
i
. Then
⋆
is a nondegenerate base point in
∨
X 1
⋯
X n
.

para.p:
Indeed, if
W i
are neighbourhoods admitting strong deformation retracts
H i
, then defining
: H
→
×
(
⨆ i
W i
)
I
⨆ i
W i
induces the desired strong deformation retract. ∎

p.text:
If
X 1
, ⋯ ,
X n
are spaces with nondegenerate base points
p i
. The map

Math.text: Phi colon bigstar _ i * pi _ 1 * open-interval@(X _ i, p _ i) rightarrow pi _ 1 * (X _ 1 or cdots * X _ n)

p.text:
induced by the inclusion
:
i j
↪
X j
∨
X 1
⋯
X n
is an isomorphism.

para.p:
We prove this for the case of two spaces. We want to be able to take
= U
X 1
,
= V
X 2
, but the problem is that these are not open. So, if
W i
are neighbourhood of
p i
admitting strong deformation retracts onto
p i
, we consider
=
U 1
⁢ q
(
⊔
X 1
W 2
)
,
=
U 2
⁢ q
(
⊔
X 2
W 1
)
where
q
is the canonical map
→
⊔
X 1
X 2
∨
X 1
X 2
. Further, the inclusions

Math.text: formulae@(set@(star) hookrightarrow U _ 1 intersection U _ 2 * X _ 1 hookrightarrow U _ 1, X _ 2 hookrightarrow U _ 2)

para.p:
are homotopy equivalences. Furthermore,
∩
U 1
U 2
is contractible hence simply connected, so that

Math.text: ((pi _ 1 * open-interval@(U _ 1, star)) star pi _ 1) * open-interval@(U _ 2, star) rightarrow pi _ factorial * open-interval@(X _ 1 or X _ 2, star)

para.p:
is an isomorphism by the Van Kampen Theorem. But since
i 1
,
i 2
were homotopy equivalences, we have isomorphisms
→
⁢
π 1
(
X i
,
p i
)
⁢
π 1
(
U i
, ⋆ )
. By induction, we are done. ∎
